narrative: Your law school application was denied because our predictive model, which assesses the likelihood of passing the bar exam on the first try, estimated a 40% chance that you would not pass—higher than the threshold we use for admissions decisions. This prediction draws from your academic record, including your undergraduate GPA of 2.90 and LSAT score of 25, both below the averages of 3.24 and 37.27 seen among students who successfully passed the bar. It also considered your Year 1 and Year 3 school decile rankings of 2, compared to averages around 6 and 5.9 for those who passed, along with your middle family income bracket and plans to attend full-time. Among the factors influencing this outcome, your undergraduate GPA stood out as the most significant positive force, suggesting it helped pull the prediction toward a higher chance of passing despite other concerns. Next in importance was your Year 3 decile ranking, which unfortunately pushed the prediction more toward the risk of failing, as it indicates performance in the lower range relative to successful bar takers. Your family income in the middle bracket also played a supportive role, nudging the outlook slightly toward passing, since a notable portion—about 35%—of those who passed came from similar backgrounds. Overall, while elements like your full-time status align well with the 93% of passers who chose that path, the combination tipped the balance toward caution. This isn't a judgment on your potential or background as a Native American woman pursuing law full-time; it's a data-driven estimate to help the school select applicants with the strongest projected bar outcomes. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to boost your score closer to the typical successful range, strengthening your undergraduate record through additional coursework or a post-bac program, and gathering strong letters of recommendation that highlight your resilience and growth. Reapplying after addressing these areas could significantly shift your predicted odds, and many students in similar positions have succeeded by focusing on these steps. We're here to support your journey toward law school.
